title: Psalms 99
subtitle: The LORD Reigns; Let the Nations Tremble

heb.paragraph: "The LORD reigns, let the nations tremble. He is great in Zion; he is exalted over all the nations. Let them praise your great and awesome name — he is holy (qādōsh)!" (vv.1–3). The refrain "he is holy" appears three times (vv.3, 5, 9) — the trisagion (triple holy). The King loves justice (v.4); he has established equity and executed justice in Jacob (v.4). "Exalt the LORD our God and worship at his footstool; he is holy" (v.5).

notes.note: "The LORD reigns. He is great in Zion, exalted over all the nations. He is HOLY." The third enthronement psalm. The distinctive addition: holiness. The reigning God is the holy God.
"The King is mighty, he loves justice. Exalt the LORD and worship at his footstool; he is holy." Power + justice + holiness. The triad that defines God’s reign.

refs.note: The seraphim's 'holy, holy, holy' in Isaiah's vision parallels the psalm's triple 'he is holy' refrain.
Living creatures saying 'holy, holy, holy' day and night continues the psalm's tradition.
God speaking from between the cherubim provides context for the psalm's 'enthroned between cherubim.'

hist.context: Psalm 99 is the climax of the enthronement collection (93-99). YHWH sits enthroned between the cherubim — the ark's lid where divine presence resided. The triple refrain 'he is holy' (vv.3, 5, 9) structures the psalm into three sections. Unlike some enthronement psalms emphasizing power, this one stresses holiness.

sections.header: Verses 6–9 — “He Answered Them; He Was a Forgiving God”

heb.paragraph: "You were to Israel a forgiving (nōsēʾ) God, though you punished their misdeeds" (v.8). Moses, Aaron, and Samuel called on God and he answered (v.6). He spoke from the pillar of cloud (v.7). The remarkable combination: God who forgives AND punishes. Mercy and discipline are not opposites but companions. "Exalt the LORD our God and worship at his holy mountain, for the LORD our God is holy" (v.9). Third "holy" — the trisagion complete.

notes.note: "Moses and Aaron were among his priests, Samuel among those who called on his name. They called on the LORD and he answered them. He spoke to them from the pillar of cloud." Named people who prayed and were answered. The God of history is the God of prayer.
"You were a forgiving God to them, though you punished their misdeeds." Forgiveness and discipline coexist. God does not choose between mercy and justice; he exercises both.
"Exalt the LORD our God and worship at his holy mountain, for the LORD our God is holy." The third and final "holy." The trisagion: holy (v.3), holy (v.5), holy (v.9). Three declarations for one God.

refs.note: God speaking to Moses face to face as to a friend provides background for the psalm's intercessor tradition.
Samuel called by name and answering parallels the psalm's 'they called and he answered.'
Christ always living to intercede fulfills the intercessory role Moses, Aaron, and Samuel held.

hist.context: The examples of Moses, Aaron, and Samuel (vv.6-8) recall great intercessors who 'called on the LORD and he answered.' God spoke to them from the pillar of cloud. The tension between forgiving God and avenging-misdeeds God (v.8) holds together mercy and justice without resolution — a mystery at the heart of divine holiness.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: The God of history is the God of prayer…

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVHe spoke to them from the pillar of cloud; they kept his statutes and the decrees he gave them.ESVIn the pillar of the cloud he spoke to them; they kept his testimonies and the statute that he gave them.NIVMoses and Aaron were among his priests, Samuel was among those who called on his name; they called on the Lord and he answered them.ESVMoses and Aaron were among his priests, Samuel also was among those who called upon his name. They called to the LORD, and he answered them.

rec.text: ""The LORD reigns. He is great in Zion, exalted over all the nations. He is HOLY." The third enthronement psalm. The distinctive addition: holiness. The reigning God is the holy God."

rows.label: Forgiving and Punishing — 99:6–9
rows.text: Moses, Aaron, Samuel answered. "A FORGIVING God, though you PUNISHED." Holy, holy, holy.

hebtext.note: "The LORD reigns, let the nations tremble. He is great in Zion; he is exalted over all the nations. Let them praise your great and awesome name — he is holy (qādōsh)!" (vv.1–3). The refrain "he is holy" appears three times (vv.3, 5, 9) — the trisagion (triple holy). The King loves justice (v.4); he has established equity and executed justice in Jacob (v.4). "Exalt the LORD our God and worship at his footstool; he is holy" (v.5).

"You were to Israel a forgiving (nōsēʾ) God, though you punished their misdeeds" (v.8). Moses, Aaron, and Samuel called on God and he answered (v.6). He spoke from the pillar of cloud (v.7). The remarkable combination: God who forgives AND punishes. Mercy and discipline are not opposites but companions. "Exalt the LORD our God and worship at his holy mountain, for the LORD our God is holy" (v.9). Third "holy" — the trisagion complete.

themes.note: Holiness peaks at 10: the trisagion — three times "holy." Sovereignty at 9: the LORD reigns. Mercy: forgiving God who also disciplines. The holiness psalm.
